description_blurb:
Apollo 12 was the fourth crewed U.S. mission to the Moon and the second to land astronauts on the lunar surface. This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 12 Technical Air-to-Ground Voice Transcription, November 1969, highlighting two periods in which astronauts reported observing unidentified phenomenon: a one hour period on the fifth day, and a two minute period on the sixth day. These transcripts contain contemporaneous observations by the flight crew reacting to unidentified phenomenon.
•	Day 05, Hour 19, Minute 14, Second 58 through Day 05, Hour 20, Minute 12, Second 14:
o	At 05:19:27:25, the pilot of the Lunar Module (LMP-LM), Astronaut Alan L. Bean, described observing particles and flashes of light “sailing off in space” via the onboard Alignment Optical Telescope (AOT). He characterized these phenomenon as “escaping the Moon.”
•	Day 06, Hour 00, Minute 21, Second 42 through Day 06, Hour 00, Minute 23, Second 33:
o	Mission Commander, Charles “Pete” Conrad, described observing floating debris outside the lunar module, which had been illuminated by the module’s onboard tracking light. At 06:00:21:51, Conrad assessed that the tracking light had burnt out because he could no longer see the debris from the module.
title: NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969
summary: This document is an excerpt from the Apollo 12 Technical Air-to-Ground Voice Transcription, November 1969, highlighting two periods where astronauts reported observing unidentified phenomena. On Day 5, Lunar Module Pilot Alan L. Bean observed particles and flashes of light "sailing off in space" and "escaping the Moon" via the Alignment Optical Telescope. On Day 6, Mission Commander Charles "Pete" Conrad observed floating debris outside the lunar module, which he initially saw illuminated by the module's onboard tracking light, later assessing the light had burned out.
reported_object_description: particles of light, flashes of light, little bits and pieces floating along with us, floating debris
reported_behavior: sailing off in space, escaping the Moon, really haul out of here and just press off at the stars, floating along with us
bust_reasoning: The bust score is 75 because the primary witness (LMP-LM) explicitly considers a mundane source for the 'particles of light, flashes of light' by stating, 'I was thinking they're dropping from my water boiler.' This is further supported by CDR-LM's description of 'little bits and pieces floating along with us.' While LMP-LM also notes they 'look like some of those things are escaping the Moon' and 'really haul out of here,' suggesting motion that resists the initial mundane explanation, this is a subjective interpretation of small particles in a vacuum. There is no multi-sensor convergence on anomalous kinematics that would strongly resist the prosaic explanation of spacecraft-generated debris or effluent.
full_text:
# NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969 - Page 1

Tape 90/3
Page 742

| Time | Speaker | Dialogue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 05 19 14 58 | CC | That's affirmative. We're ready for the E-MOD. |
| 05 19 16 31 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. |
| 05 19 16 35 | CDR-LM | Go. |
| 05 19 16 37 | CC | If you will give us P00 and ACCEPT, we'll give you a CSM state vector and RLS update. |
| 05 19 16 45 | CDR-LM | You have P00 and ACCEPT. |
| 05 19 20 05 | CMP | Hello, Houston; Yankee Clipper. |
| 05 19 20 09 | CC | Yankee Clipper, Houston. Loud and clear. |
| 05 19 20 14 | CMP | Well, hello there, stranger. How are you? |
| 05 19 20 22 | CC | Morning, Dick. We are fine. How are you? |
| 05 19 20 27 | CMP | Well, pretty good. I hope you would like to have some company for a change. |
| 05 19 20 31 | CC | Roger. Got the house clean? |
| 05 19 20 36 | CMP | As a matter of fact, I just finished that. I sure do; got everything in order; ready to go towards the LM and bring back ... That's quite a chore; keeping this thing clean. |
| 05 19 20 53 | CC | Roger. You got a couple of coal miners coming up to see you. |
| 05 19 20 59 | CMP | That's okay. I'll be glad to see them. |
| 05 19 21 10 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. The computer is yours. Break. Yankee Clipper, if you will go P00 and ACCEPT, we have an uplink. |
| 05 19 21 20 | CMP | All yours. |
| 05 19 23 14 | CDR-LM | Houston, you got the lift-off time for me? |
| 05 19 23 20 | CC | Stand by. |
| 05 19 23 39 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. Your lift-off time is 142:03:47. |
| 05 19 23 52 | CDR-LM | I copy 142:03:47.00. |
| 05 19 23 57 | CC | Affirmative. |
| 05 19 24 05 | CC | Clipper, Houston. Computer's yours. |



# NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969 - Page 2

**Document title:** NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969
**Source file:** NASA-UAP-D1_ Apollo 12 Transcript_ 1969.pdf
**Page:** 2 of 4
**Agency:** NASA
**Release date:** 5/8/26
**Dataset description:** Incident date: 1969; Incident location: Moon

***

Tape 90/4
Page 743

| Time | Speaker | Transcript |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 05 19 24 11 | CMP | Okay. And Jerry, will you find out what they want to do about this battery charge, because I'm using the bus ties during the rendezvous? |
| 05 19 24 23 | CC | Roger. |
| 05 19 24 43 | CC | Yankee Clipper, Houston. Why don't you figure on terminating the battery charge at LOS? |
| 05 19 24 52 | CMP | All right; I could let it go until I ... just before lift-off. That way it might take it all the way up. |
| 05 19 25 33 | CC | Clipper, Houston. We prefer that you terminate at LOS on this pass. |
| 05 19 25 40 | CMP | Roger. |
| 05 19 25 41 | CC | Roger. That would be one less thing for us to keep track of prior to lift-off. |
| 05 19 25 48 | CMP | Okay. |
| 05 19 27 17 | CDR-LM | Say, Houston; Intrepid. |
| 05 19 27 20 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. Go. |
| 05 19 27 25 | LMP-LM | Roger. When you look out the AOT in the dark quadrant? You can see these lights - particles of light, flashes of light just seem to come from - in this case, I'm looking in quadrant 1 which is the left one. It's coming from behind me, the left, and they're just sailing off in space. I was thinking they're dropping from my water boiler, but it looks like some of those things are escaping the Moon. They really haul out of here and just press off at the stars. |
| 05 19 27 56 | CC | Roger. |
| 05 19 28 25 | CC | Yankee Clipper, Houston with a P22 tracking PAD. |
| 05 19 28 42 | CMP | Go ahead. |
| 05 19 28 44 | CC | Roger. Your target is LM; T1 is 139:57:39; T2 is 140:02:38; south 05; latitude is minus 3 - - |
| 05 19 29 10 | CMP | Roger. T 112 - |



# NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969 - Page 3

Tape 90/9
Page 748

| Time | Speaker | Text |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 05 20 08 23 | CC | Clipper, Houston. We'll give that data a good evaluation before we do anything with it. |
| 05 20 09 25 | LMP-LM | Houston, Intrepid. |
| 05 20 09 30 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. GO. |
| 05 20 09 34 | LMP-LM | Got sort of an interesting thing going on AGS right now. I didn't notice earlier, but it may just be because the lights are brighter now. I'm getting an all 8's flash on both the address and the information registers at about one-fifth the brilliance of the normal numbers. And a - It's pulsing every second. |
| 05 20 10 00 | CC | Roger, Al. |
| 05 20 10 06 | LMP-LM | If I turn down the illumination level just a little bit, it's not noticeable. |
| 05 20 10 52 | LMP-LM | Hello, Houston; Intrepid. You ready for my RCS hot fire? |
| 05 20 10 59 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. Roger. Fire away. |
| 05 20 11 03 | CDR-LM | Okay. |
| 05 20 11 32 | CC | Intrepid, Houston. |
| 05 20 11 37 | LMP-LM | Go. |
| 05 20 11 39 | CC | Roger, Al. Fredo is here. He and I have both seen that phenomena on your DEDA during testing of most all the spacecrafts up at Bethpage, and it's probably an EMI. |
| 05 20 11 56 | CDR-LM | That's what we've been talking about, but we thought we'd touch in on it. |
| 05 20 11 59 | LMP-LM | When you go to your roll rate, roll left, pitch up - - |
| 05 20 12 01 | CC | Roger. I think TRW's got a workup on this problem. |
| 05 20 12 08 | CDR-LM | Okay? |
| 05 20 12 11 | CDR-LM | Here you go, Houston, with roll, pitch, and yaw. |
| 05 20 12 14 | CC | Roger, Pete. |



# NASA-UAP-D1, Apollo 12 Transcript, 1969 - Page 4

Tape 93/8
Page 778

| Time | Speaker | Text |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 06 00 21 42 | CMP | But I don't have you in the sextant. That's okay. Your blinking light's just not blinking, that's all. |
| 06 CO 21 51 | CDR-LM | Hey, Houston. It looks like our tracking light's burned out. Dick hasn't been able to find us in this sextant. And on the first nightside pass we had little bits and pieces floating along with us and we could tell that the tracking light was flashing on them. And we still have, I've presumed to think, bits and pieces floating along and nothing's flashing on them, so I'm pretty sure it burned out. |
| 06 00 22 11 | CC | Roger, Pete. |
| 06 00 22 22 | LMP-LM | Yes, sir. Okay. |
| 06 00 22 26 | CC | Hi, Intrepid. |
| 06 00 22 27 | LMP-LM | Okay. |
| 06 00 22 28 | CC | This is Houston. How'd your sweepdown fore and aft go? |
| 06 00 22 33 | CDR-LM | It's getting much cleaner in here running this way; and, also, Yankee Clipper informs me he has the television all set up. When we come around the horn, we'll come around with the television on in VOX. |
| 06 00 22 47 | CC | Roger. |
| 06 00 22 53 | CDR-LM | Who knows, you may get to see the first whifferdill. |
| 06 00 22 59 | CC | Roger, Pete. Our electrical watchers say that the current indicates that your tracking light is on. |
| 06 00 23 11 | CDR-LM | Okay. Now we just turned it off. Now does the current show that? |
| 06 00 23 19 | CC | It - It sure does, Pete. |
| 06 00 23 26 | CMP | You're - they're - You're flying through the air backwards, then, Pete, because I don't see it. |
| 06 00 23 33 | CDR-LM | Well, my ball tells me I'm pointed at you, Dick, and so does my radar. |
cover_up_reasoning: No concealment is evident in the provided transcript. The conversation about the observed phenomena is open and direct between the crew and ground control. There are no redactions, classified references, or indications of suppressed information.
summary_zh: 本文件摘录自1969年11月《阿波罗12号技术天地通话记录》，重点阐述宇航员报告观测到不明现象的两个时段。在第5天，登月舱驾驶员艾伦·L·比恩通过对准光学望远镜观测到光粒子和闪光“在太空中飘离”并“脱离月球”。在第6天，任务指令长查尔斯·“皮特”·康拉德观测到登月舱外部有漂浮碎片，他最初看到这些碎片被登月舱的机载跟踪灯照亮，随后判断该灯已烧毁。
reported_object_description_zh: 光粒子，闪光，与我们一同漂浮的小碎片，漂浮碎片
reported_behavior_zh: 在太空中飘离，脱离月球，真正地从这里加速离开并冲向星辰，与我们一同漂浮
bust_reasoning_zh: 驳斥评分为75，原因在于主要目击者（登月舱驾驶员）明确考虑了“光粒子、闪光”的普通来源，他表示：“我当时在想它们是不是从我的热水器里掉出来的。”登月舱指令长对“与我们一同漂浮的小碎片”的描述进一步支持了这一点。尽管登月舱驾驶员也指出它们“看起来有些东西正在脱离月球”并“真正地从这里加速离开”，这暗示了某种运动，似乎与最初的普通解释相悖，但这只是对真空中小颗粒的主观解读。没有多传感器数据汇聚表明存在异常运动学特征，能够有力驳斥航天器产生碎片或排放物的平淡解释。
cover_up_reasoning_zh: 在所提供的记录中，没有发现任何隐瞒迹象。关于所观测现象的对话在宇航员与地面控制中心之间是公开且直接的。没有删节、机密引用或信息被压制的迹象。
claims: LMP-LM Alan L. Bean observed particles and flashes of light "sailing off in space" and "escaping the Moon" through the Alignment Optical Telescope (AOT).
CDR-LM Charles "Pete" Conrad observed floating debris outside the lunar module.
The observed debris was initially seen illuminated by the LM's onboard tracking light.
Conrad assessed that the tracking light had burned out because he could no longer see the debris illuminated.
claims_zh: 登月舱驾驶员艾伦·L·比恩通过对准光学望远镜（AOT）观测到光粒子和闪光“在太空中飘离”并“脱离月球”。
登月舱指令长查尔斯·“皮特”·康拉德观测到登月舱外部有漂浮碎片。
所观测到的碎片最初被登月舱的机载跟踪灯照亮。
康拉德判断跟踪灯已烧毁，因为他无法再看到被照亮的碎片。
bust_explanations: Spacecraft effluent or debris (e.g., ice crystals, small particles ejected from the water boiler or other systems). The astronaut explicitly considers this possibility, stating, "I was thinking they're dropping from my water boiler," and later refers to "little bits and pieces floating along with us."
Optical effects or reflections within the Alignment Optical Telescope (AOT) or sextant, possibly exacerbated by the dark quadrant or the specific lighting conditions in space.
Micrometeoroids or natural space dust illuminated by sunlight. While less likely given the 'floating along with us' description, it remains a possibility for small, transient flashes of light.
bust_explanations_zh: 航天器排放物或碎片（例如，冰晶、从热水器或其他系统喷出的小颗粒）。宇航员明确考虑了这种可能性，他表示：“我当时在想它们是不是从我的热水器里掉出来的”，随后又提到“与我们一同漂浮的小碎片”。
对准光学望远镜（AOT）或六分仪内部的光学效应或反射，可能因黑暗象限或太空中特定照明条件而加剧。
被阳光照亮的微陨石或天然太空尘埃。尽管考虑到“与我们一同漂浮”的描述，这种可能性较低，但对于小型、瞬时闪光而言，它仍然是一种可能性。
